title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Understanding how people move through cities is a core problem in urban planning and transportation research. GPS trajectories from smartphones capture rich movement data, but they don’t directly tell us how someone is moving — whether they’re walking, cycling, or sitting on a bus. This label has to be inferred from the movement itself.
In this assignment I work with the GeoLife dataset (Zheng et al., 2011), a collection of GPS trajectories from 182 users recorded in Beijing between 2007 and 2012. A labelled subset of 73 users includes transportation mode annotations — walking, cycling, bus, car, subway, and train. The task is to build a model that can predict the transportation mode for each individual GPS point in the unlabelled validation dataset.
This is a harder version of what we did in Advanced Environmental Statistics: instead of summarising features per segment and predicting one label per track, here every point needs its own prediction. That means the features have to capture local movement behaviour at the point level — which is actually much more interesting.

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Understanding how people move through cities is a core problem in urban planning and transportation research. GPS trajectories from smartphones capture rich movement data, but they don’t directly tell us how someone is moving — whether they’re walking, cycling, or sitting on a bus. This label has to be inferred from the movement itself.
In this assignment I work with the GeoLife dataset (Zheng et al., 2011), a collection of GPS trajectories from 182 users recorded in Beijing between 2007 and 2012. A labelled subset of 73 users includes transportation mode annotations — walking, cycling, bus, car, subway, and train. The task is to build a model that can predict the transportation mode for each individual GPS point in the unlabelled validation dataset.
This is a harder version of what we did in Advanced Environmental Statistics: instead of summarising features per segment and predicting one label per track, here every point needs its own prediction. That means the features have to capture local movement behaviour at the point level — which is actually much more interesting.

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Setup


Code
library(sf)          # spatial data
library(dplyr)       # data wrangling
library(ggplot2)     # plotting
library(forcats)     # factor reordering
library(zoo)         # rolling window functions
library(ranger)      # fast random forest
library(caret)       # confusion matrix / evaluation

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 1: Load the Data
The GeoPackage contains three layers — training, testing, and validation. I load all three and tag them with a data column before combining.


Code
path <- "data/tracks_1.gpkg"

training_dataset <- read_sf(path, layer = "training") |>
  mutate(data = "training")

testing_dataset <- read_sf(path, layer = "testing") |>
  mutate(data = "testing")

validation_dataset <- read_sf(path, layer = "validation") |>
  mutate(data = "validation")

full_dataset <- bind_rows(training_dataset, testing_dataset, validation_dataset)

cat("Layers loaded successfully.\n")


Layers loaded successfully.


Code
cat("Training points:  ", nrow(training_dataset), "\n")


Training points:   501432 


Code
cat("Testing points:   ", nrow(testing_dataset), "\n")


Testing points:    262851 


Code
cat("Validation points:", nrow(validation_dataset), "\n")


Validation points: 240449

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 2: Feature Engineering
Feature engineering is where most of the work happens. Raw GPS points just have coordinates and a timestamp — everything else has to be computed. I derive a set of movement parameters that capture different aspects of how a person is moving at each point.

Base parameters: speed and time lag
Step length (distance between consecutive points) and time lag (time between consecutive timestamps) are the fundamental building blocks. Speed follows directly.


Code
full_dataset <- full_dataset |>
  mutate(
    steplength = as.numeric(st_distance(lead(geom), geom, by_element = TRUE)),
    timelag    = as.numeric(difftime(lead(datetime), datetime, units = "secs")),
    speed      = steplength / timelag,
    .by = track_id
  )




Acceleration
Acceleration captures whether a person is speeding up or slowing down. Walking and cycling have much more variable acceleration than, say, taking a train.


Code
full_dataset <- full_dataset |>
  mutate(
    acceleration = (lead(speed) - speed) / timelag,
    .by = track_id
  )




Turning angle
The turning angle between consecutive movement steps is another useful signal. Pedestrians and cyclists tend to make more frequent direction changes than vehicles following roads or rail lines. I compute the bearing of each step and then the absolute angular difference between consecutive steps.


Code
# Extract coordinates for bearing calculation
coords <- st_coordinates(full_dataset)

full_dataset <- full_dataset |>
  mutate(
    x = coords[, 1],
    y = coords[, 2]
  )

full_dataset <- full_dataset |>
  mutate(
    dx        = lead(x) - x,
    dy        = lead(y) - y,
    bearing   = atan2(dx, dy),          # radians
    turn_angle = abs(bearing - lag(bearing)),
    # Wrap to [0, pi]
    turn_angle = ifelse(turn_angle > pi, 2 * pi - turn_angle, turn_angle),
    .by = track_id
  )




Sinuosity (two window sizes)
Sinuosity — the ratio of actual path length to straight-line distance — measures path curvature. I compute it over two different window sizes (5 and 10 points) to capture both local and slightly broader movement patterns.


Code
full_dataset <- full_dataset |>
  mutate(
    # 5-point window
    straight_dist5 = as.numeric(st_distance(lead(geom, 5), geom, by_element = TRUE)),
    full_dist5     = rollsum(steplength, 5, fill = NA, align = "left"),
    sinuosity5     = full_dist5 / straight_dist5,

    # 10-point window
    straight_dist10 = as.numeric(st_distance(lead(geom, 10), geom, by_element = TRUE)),
    full_dist10     = rollsum(steplength, 10, fill = NA, align = "left"),
    sinuosity10     = full_dist10 / straight_dist10,

    .by = track_id
  )




Speed distribution check
Before training, it’s worth looking at how speed distributes across modes. This confirms that speed alone already carries a lot of discriminative power.


Code
full_dataset |>
  filter(data %in% c("training", "testing"), !is.na(mode)) |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  filter(speed < 30, speed > 0) |>
  mutate(mode = fct_reorder(mode, speed, .fun = median, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(speed, mode, fill = mode)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    x = "Speed (m/s)",
    y = "Transportation mode",
    title = "Per-point speed by transportation mode"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")





Distribution of per-point speed by transportation mode (training + testing data only; extreme outliers removed for clarity).




Walking and cycling cluster at low speeds, while car, bus, and train spread across higher values. Subway is interestingly variable — underground sections have consistent high speed, but stops bring it to zero. This overlap between modes is exactly why we need multiple features, not just speed alone.

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 3: Prepare Data for Modelling
For per-point prediction I work directly with the point-level features — no aggregation. I select the relevant columns, remove rows with NA values in the features (these arise at segment boundaries due to lead()/lag() and rolling windows), and split back into training, testing, and validation sets.


Code
feature_cols <- c("speed", "acceleration", "turn_angle",
                  "sinuosity5", "sinuosity10", "steplength", "timelag")

# Point-level dataset (drop geometry, keep only needed columns)
points_df <- full_dataset |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(data, track_id, mode, all_of(feature_cols))

# Remove rows where any feature is NA or infinite
points_df <- points_df |>
  filter(if_all(all_of(feature_cols), ~ !is.na(.) & is.finite(.)))

# Split (NA-removed versions for training/testing)
points_training   <- points_df |> filter(data == "training")   |> select(-data, -track_id) |> mutate(mode = factor(mode))
points_testing    <- points_df |> filter(data == "testing")     |> select(-data, -track_id) |> mutate(mode = factor(mode))
points_validation <- points_df |> filter(data == "validation")  |> select(-data, -track_id)

cat("Training points (after NA removal):   ", nrow(points_training), "\n")


Training points (after NA removal):    393368 


Code
cat("Testing points (after NA removal):    ", nrow(points_testing), "\n")


Testing points (after NA removal):     212129 


Code
cat("Validation points (after NA removal): ", nrow(points_validation), "\n")


Validation points (after NA removal):  178034 


Code
# For validation: keep ALL 240k points by imputing boundary NAs with
# training medians — standard practice for segment-boundary artefacts.
train_medians <- points_training |>
  summarise(across(all_of(feature_cols), \(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE)))

points_validation_full <- full_dataset |>
  filter(data == "validation") |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(all_of(feature_cols)) |>
  mutate(across(all_of(feature_cols), \(x) {
    m <- train_medians[[cur_column()]]
    ifelse(is.na(x) | !is.finite(x), m, x)
  }))

cat("Validation points (full, imputed):    ", nrow(points_validation_full), "\n")


Validation points (full, imputed):     240449

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 4: Train a Random Forest
I use a Random Forest via the ranger package. Random Forests handle the class imbalance and feature interactions in this dataset much better than a single decision tree — which is important here because modes like train and subway are quite rare compared to walk.


Code
set.seed(42)

rf_model <- ranger(
  formula        = mode ~ .,
  data           = points_training,
  num.trees      = 300,
  importance     = "impurity",
  num.threads    = 2,
  classification = TRUE
)


Growing trees.. Progress: 10%. Estimated remaining time: 4 minutes, 37 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 17%. Estimated remaining time: 5 minutes, 17 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 26%. Estimated remaining time: 4 minutes, 36 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 34%. Estimated remaining time: 4 minutes, 12 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 42%. Estimated remaining time: 3 minutes, 42 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 49%. Estimated remaining time: 3 minutes, 15 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 59%. Estimated remaining time: 2 minutes, 34 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 66%. Estimated remaining time: 2 minutes, 9 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 73%. Estimated remaining time: 1 minute, 46 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 78%. Estimated remaining time: 1 minute, 29 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 86%. Estimated remaining time: 56 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 92%. Estimated remaining time: 35 seconds.
Growing trees.. Progress: 96%. Estimated remaining time: 16 seconds.


Code
cat("OOB prediction error (training):", round(rf_model$prediction.error * 100, 2), "%\n")


OOB prediction error (training): 34.52 %

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 4b: CART for Comparison
To see how much the Random Forest actually gains over a single tree, I train a CART model on exactly the same per-point features and evaluate it on the same testing data.


Code
library(rpart)

cart_model <- rpart(
  mode ~ .,
  data   = points_training,
  method = "class"
)




Code
# CART predictions on testing set
pred_cart   <- predict(cart_model, newdata = points_testing, type = "class")
lvls_c      <- sort(unique(as.character(points_testing$mode)))
cm_cart     <- confusionMatrix(factor(pred_cart, levels = lvls_c),
                               reference = factor(points_testing$mode, levels = lvls_c))

# RF predictions on testing set (computed here for the comparison table)
pred_rf_cmp <- predict(rf_model, data = points_testing)$predictions
cm_rf_cmp   <- confusionMatrix(factor(pred_rf_cmp, levels = lvls_c),
                               reference = factor(points_testing$mode, levels = lvls_c))

# Side-by-side comparison
comparison <- data.frame(
  Model    = c("CART (single tree)", "Random Forest (300 trees)"),
  Accuracy = c(
    round(cm_cart$overall["Accuracy"] * 100, 2),
    round(cm_rf_cmp$overall["Accuracy"] * 100, 2)
  ),
  Kappa = c(
    round(cm_cart$overall["Kappa"], 3),
    round(cm_rf_cmp$overall["Kappa"], 3)
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  comparison,
  col.names = c("Model", "Accuracy (%)", "Kappa"),
  caption   = "Accuracy comparison: CART vs. Random Forest on per-point testing data."
)



Accuracy comparison: CART vs. Random Forest on per-point testing data.


Model
Accuracy (%)
Kappa




CART (single tree)
54.22
0.392


Random Forest (300 trees)
61.71
0.490





The Random Forest clearly outperforms the single CART on both metrics. The gap is particularly visible for rare classes — the ensemble averaging in a Random Forest stabilises predictions for underrepresented modes like train and subway.

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 5: Evaluate on Testing Data


Code
# Predict on testing set
pred_test <- predict(rf_model, data = points_testing)$predictions

# Align factor levels
lvls <- sort(unique(as.character(points_testing$mode)))
pred_factor <- factor(pred_test, levels = lvls)
true_factor <- factor(points_testing$mode, levels = lvls)

cm <- confusionMatrix(pred_factor, reference = true_factor)
print(cm)


Confusion Matrix and Statistics

Code
# Build a tidy confusion matrix for ggplot
cm_df <- as.data.frame(cm$table)

ggplot(cm_df, aes(x = Reference, y = Prediction, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix – Random Forest (per-point)",
    x = "True mode",
    y = "Predicted mode",
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))





Confusion matrix for the Random Forest model on the testing data. Colour intensity reflects the count of predictions in each cell.





Feature importance


Code
imp_df <- data.frame(
  feature    = names(rf_model$variable.importance),
  importance = rf_model$variable.importance
) |>
  arrange(importance)

imp_df$feature <- factor(imp_df$feature, levels = imp_df$feature)

ggplot(imp_df, aes(x = importance, y = feature)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c7bb6", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Feature importance (Random Forest)",
    x     = "Mean decrease in node impurity",
    y     = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Variable importance (impurity-based) for the Random Forest model.




Speed and step length dominate as expected, but turning angle and sinuosity provide meaningful additional signal — especially for distinguishing walking and cycling from motorised transport.

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Step 6: Predict the Validation Data
The validation dataset has no mode labels, so I predict and attach the results. I use the full imputed validation set (all 240’449 points) — boundary points where rolling-window features would be NA are filled with training medians, so no point is left without a prediction.


Code
pred_val <- predict(rf_model, data = points_validation_full)$predictions

# Summary
pred_summary <- as.data.frame(table(prediction = pred_val)) |>
  arrange(desc(Freq))

knitr::kable(
  pred_summary,
  col.names = c("Predicted mode", "Count"),
  caption   = "Distribution of predicted transportation modes in the full validation dataset (all 240'449 points)."
)



Distribution of predicted transportation modes in the full validation dataset (all 240’449 points).


Predicted mode
Count

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
Discussion
The Random Forest model reaches an overall accuracy of 61.7 % on the per-point testing data, compared to 54.2 % for the single CART on the same task (see comparison table). Both are below the ~69 % the CART baseline achieved in the Advanced Environmental Statistics assignment — but that comparison isn’t fair. The AES CART predicted one label per segment using aggregated features (mean, max, min), which is a much easier task. Here every single GPS point gets its own prediction from raw local features, with no smoothing over a whole trajectory. The Random Forest’s advantage over the single tree is most visible for rare classes like train and subway, where the ensemble averaging stabilises predictions that a single tree gets wrong.
The model struggles most with bus vs. car and subway vs. train, which is visible in the confusion matrix. That’s not particularly surprising: from a kinematics perspective, sitting in a bus and sitting in a car feel almost identical. Both move at similar speeds along roads, neither produces a distinctive turning-angle signature, and the stops of a bus are too brief to register clearly in a 5–10 point sinuosity window. Distinguishing them properly would require context — road network type, stop frequency, distance to rail — which is exactly what the upcoming CMA2 lecture on context-aware movement analysis addresses.
Walking is the best-predicted class with a sensitivity of ~81 %, while cycling reaches ~63 %. The gap between them reflects the value of adding turning angle and dual-window sinuosity on top of speed — pedestrians genuinely do take more winding paths and change direction more often than cyclists, and those features capture that difference.
The OOB training error of ~34.5 % is a few points better than the ~38.3 % test error, which means the model generalises reasonably — it’s not dramatically overfitting. The main weak point is the rare classes: train has a sensitivity of only 31 % because it’s so underrepresented in training. Giving those classes higher weight or using oversampling (SMOTE) would be a logical next step.
For the final validation predictions, boundary points where rolling-window features (sinuosity, acceleration) were undefined due to segment edges are imputed with training medians. This ensures all 240’449 validation points receive a prediction rather than being silently dropped.

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
References
Zheng, Yu, Hao Fu, Xing Xie, Wei-Ying Ma, and Quannan Li. 2011. Geolife GPS Trajectory Dataset – User Guide. Geolife GPS trajectories 1.1. https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/geolife-gps-trajectory-dataset-user-guide/

title: Movement Analysis I
text:
AI Declaration
Parts of this assignment were developed with the assistance of an AI language model (Claude, Anthropic). Specifically, I used AI support to help structure the per-point feature engineering pipeline, to debug issues with NA removal before model training, and to review the code logic for turning angle computation. Everything has been checked and validated by me.
